preamble: The shift from the voiceless alveolar stop [t] to its voiced counterpart [d] is a primary form of lenition. It typically occurs in intervocalic positions where the vocal folds continue to vibrate through the consonant, assimilating the stop to the surrounding vowels.

examples.note: Latin intervocalic [t] voices to [d] in Spanish.
French historical voicing [t] > [d], then further lenition and elision.
Intervocalic flapping/voicing: /t/ becomes a voiced alveolar tap.
Consonant gradation weak grade: word-medial /t/ voices to [d] in closed syllable context (nominative vs. oblique).
Systematic intervocalic voicing in weak grade of consonant gradation paradigm.
Intervocalic voicing: /t/ is realized as [d] between voiced sounds.

sources: Penny: A History of the Spanish Language
